ontology.foodex2_description: Cabbage (Brassica oleracea var. capitata), raw, white/green

main_ingredients: white cabbage (Brassica oleracea var. capitata), raw

serving.natural_language_portion: 1 cup of shredded raw cabbage weighs approximately 90g. A standard stir-fry portion uses approximately 150–200g raw cabbage.

nutrient_source.title: White Cabbage (raw) — HPB Singapore Foodo Database, Food Code F14010019; cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB), Singapore; USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Exact match in HPB Foodo under food code F14010019 — White Cabbage, raw.

gi_source: GI not applicable — cabbage has minimal digestible starch. Despite having 5.8g/100g carbohydrate, most of this is dietary fibre and sugars with a very low glycaemic impact.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (18mg/100g) and low calorie. Cabbage is a cruciferous vegetable containing glucosinolates which convert to isothiocyanates and may have mild cardioprotective properties. Very good vitamin C content (36.6mg/100g) supporting vascular endothelial function.
hypertension.guidance: Cabbage is an excellent affordable vegetable for patients with hypertension. No sodium concerns from the raw vegetable itself. Recommended in soups, stir-fries, and salads. Avoid high-sodium preparations (kimchi-style heavily salted cabbage, cabbage with oyster sauce) that reverse the sodium benefit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low calorie (25 kcal/100g) with useful dietary fibre (2.5g/100g). The fibre slows digestion and improves overall meal glycaemic response. Folate (43mcg DFE/100g) supports cell metabolism.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Cabbage is an excellent high-volume, low-calorie vegetable for patients with type 2 diabetes. Including 200–300g of cabbage and other low-starch vegetables per meal displaces rice/noodles and dramatically reduces the meal's glycaemic load. Raw cabbage in coleslaw (with minimal dressing) is a useful salad base.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Cruciferous vegetables (cabbage, broccoli, cauliflower) contain sulforaphane (from glucosinolate metabolism) which inhibits NF-κB — a key inflammatory signalling pathway. Vitamin C (36.6mg/100g) supports collagen synthesis.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Cabbage and other cruciferous vegetables are recommended for patients with rheumatoid arthritis for their anti-inflammatory sulforaphane content. Raw or lightly cooked cabbage provides more glucosinolates than overcooked preparations. Include daily in the diet.

llm_training.natural_language_description: White round cabbage (Brassica oleracea var. capitata) is the most commonly purchased cabbage variety in Singapore households and hawker stalls. Very low calorie (25 kcal/100g), very high water content (92.2%), and a good source of vitamin C (36.6mg/100g) and folate (43mcg DFE/100g). A cruciferous vegetable — member of the same family as broccoli, cauliflower, and bok choy. Contains glucosinolates which are metabolised to bioactive isothiocyanates (including sulforaphane) with anti-inflammatory and potential cancer-protective properties. Used in Singapore char kway teow, economy rice (cheap vegetable option), coleslaw, soups, dumplings, and okonomiyaki.

confidence_reason: Exact match in HPB Singapore Foodo Database (F14010019).
notes: Values per 100g raw white cabbage, outer leaves removed. Cooking reduces vitamin C content significantly (50–60% loss with prolonged boiling). Raw or lightly stir-fried cabbage preserves more glucosinolates and vitamin C than overcooked preparations.
